stem:
A student is writing about community gardens. She wants to support the claim that community gardens improve neighborhood food access. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Community gardens are shared plots where residents grow vegetables, herbs, and fruit. In many urban neighborhoods, fresh produce is difficult to buy nearby. Advocates argue that gardens can fill this gap for families who live far from grocery stores.

choices.text: Community gardens are often painted bright colors to attract attention.
In neighborhoods with community gardens, residents reported eating an average of 1.4 more servings of fresh vegetables per day than residents in similar neighborhoods without gardens.
Gardening is considered a relaxing hobby by many adults.
Some community gardens host weekend art events.

explanation: The finding ties garden presence directly to higher fresh-vegetable consumption, which is the specific access claim being made.

stem:
A science teacher claims that using classroom pets, like guinea pigs, helps reduce student anxiety during tests. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Several elementary schools have begun keeping small animals in classrooms. Teachers say the pets give students something calming to focus on. Some districts are now deciding whether to expand the program.

choices.text: Guinea pigs are herbivores that primarily eat hay and pellets.
Many children enjoy watching animals at the zoo.
Caring for pets can teach students responsibility.
Students in classrooms with guinea pigs reported 30% lower self-rated test anxiety than students in classrooms without pets.

explanation: The finding directly measures the outcome in the claim — lower test anxiety — and links it to the presence of the classroom pet.

stem:
Which finding would most directly support the claim that bike lanes make cycling safer for commuters?

Passage: Many cities have installed protected bike lanes separated from car traffic by barriers. Supporters argue that these lanes prevent serious accidents. Critics say the lanes take up road space that could be used for cars.

choices.text: Bike lanes are usually painted green or red to stand out from car lanes.
Bicycles are a popular form of transportation in the Netherlands.
Streets that added protected bike lanes saw a 52% drop in injuries to cyclists compared to streets without them.
Some cyclists prefer road bikes with thin tires.

explanation: A measured drop in cyclist injuries on lane-equipped streets directly tests and confirms the safety claim.

stem:
A researcher argues that later school start times help teenagers get more sleep. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Teenagers naturally fall asleep later than younger children because their internal clocks shift during puberty. Some districts have delayed the start of high school from 7:30 a.m. to 8:30 a.m. Others have kept the earlier schedule.

choices.text: Many teenagers use their phones before bed.
High schoolers in districts that moved to an 8:30 a.m. start reported sleeping an average of 45 minutes longer per night than high schoolers in districts that still started at 7:30 a.m.
Sleep is important for physical health.
Some teachers prefer morning classes to afternoon ones.

explanation: The finding compares sleep duration across the exact two start times named in the claim, so it directly tests the effect of later starts.

stem:
A student wants to support the claim that handwritten notes help college students remember lecture material better than typed notes. Which finding would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Many college students now type their notes on laptops instead of writing them by hand. Some professors worry that typing leads to shallow, word-for-word copying. Others say typing is simply faster and neater.

choices.text: On a week-later recall test, students who took handwritten notes answered 22% more conceptual questions correctly than students who typed their notes.
Typing speed varies widely among college students.
Laptops can be expensive for students on a budget.
Notebooks are sold in most campus bookstores.

explanation: A direct recall-test comparison between handwriters and typers measures exactly the memory advantage the claim describes.

stem:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support the claim that planting native wildflowers along highways increases local bee populations?

Passage: Some state transportation departments have started planting native wildflower strips along highway shoulders. Supporters say this creates food and habitat for pollinators. A few departments are now tracking how these strips affect insect populations.

choices.text: Wildflowers require less mowing than turfgrass.
Bees are important for agriculture worldwide.
Some wildflowers bloom in early spring.
Highway shoulders planted with native wildflowers recorded, on average, three times as many individual bees per survey as shoulders planted only with grass.

explanation: A direct count of bees on wildflower versus grass shoulders shows the population difference the claim makes.

stem:
A historian argues that public libraries became more important to small towns during the 1930s economic downturn. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: During the 1930s, many small towns in the United States saw factories close and unemployment rise. Residents needed cheap or free places to spend time, read, and learn new skills. Historians have long debated how these conditions changed daily life.

choices.text: Many libraries from this era were built with stone walls and tall windows.
Records from small-town libraries show that average annual visits per resident more than doubled between 1929 and 1935.
Library cards in many countries are now free to obtain.
Some famous writers worked in libraries.

explanation: A measured jump in library visits per resident during exactly those years is direct evidence that libraries became more central to daily life.

stem:
Which finding would most directly support the claim that group exercise classes help older adults stay physically active longer than exercising alone?

Passage: A community center offers both group fitness classes and open gym times. Staff have noticed that some members stick with their routines for years while others drop out quickly. They wonder whether the group setting itself makes a difference.

choices.text: After one year, 78% of older adults enrolled in group classes were still attending regularly, compared with 34% of those who exercised alone.
Group classes are often scheduled in the morning.
Many gyms offer a variety of equipment.
Exercise is generally recommended by doctors.

explanation: A direct one-year retention comparison between group and solo exercisers tests exactly the durability advantage named in the claim.

stem:
In a paper about urban heat, a student writes the following claim: "Street trees lower temperatures in dense city blocks during heat waves." Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: During summer heat waves, densely built city blocks can become significantly hotter than surrounding parks. Planners have proposed adding street trees to cool the air through shade and evapotranspiration. Opponents say trees take years to grow large enough to matter.

choices.text: Tree-lined blocks are often considered more attractive by residents.
During a recent heat wave, sensors showed that city blocks with mature street trees averaged 4.8°F cooler at pedestrian height than otherwise similar blocks without trees.
Trees planted in sidewalks can sometimes damage concrete with their roots.
Many cities are expanding their park systems.

explanation: The sensor comparison directly measures the temperature difference that the claim predicts — cooler blocks during heat waves — while controlling for similarity.

stem:
A nutritionist argues that offering free fruit in office break rooms leads employees to eat less candy during the workday. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Several companies have begun stocking break rooms with bowls of free apples, bananas, and oranges. Before this change, candy dishes were the main free snack. The nutritionist wants to know whether the switch changes what workers actually reach for.

choices.text: Fruit often costs less than packaged candy per serving.
After free fruit was introduced, office surveys showed that employees ate an average of 2.1 fewer pieces of candy per workday than before the change.
Many people enjoy sweet snacks in the afternoon.
Office workers sometimes bring snacks from home.

explanation: The finding measures the specific outcome named in the claim — reduced candy consumption — and links it to the introduction of free fruit.

stem:
A literary scholar writes: "Emily Dickinson's poems became much more widely read after the 1955 release of Thomas H. Johnson's complete, unedited edition." Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: For decades after Dickinson's death, her poems circulated mainly in heavily edited versions that smoothed out her unusual punctuation and capitalization. In 1955, Thomas H. Johnson published the first scholarly edition that restored the original text. Scholars still debate how much this edition changed her reputation.

choices.text: Dickinson wrote nearly 1,800 poems during her lifetime.
Circulation records from major public libraries show that Dickinson's poetry was checked out nearly four times more often in the decade after 1955 than in the decade before.
Johnson also wrote letters about his editorial choices.
Many readers today admire Dickinson's use of dashes.

explanation: Library circulation is a direct measure of how widely poems were actually read, and the before/after comparison targets the 1955 turning point the claim identifies.

stem:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support the claim that captive breeding programs have helped the California condor recover?

Passage: By 1987, only 27 California condors remained alive, all of them in captivity. Biologists launched a breeding program to raise chicks and release them back into the wild. Critics feared the released birds would fail to survive on their own.

choices.text: Condors have the largest wingspan of any North American bird.
Lead poisoning from ammunition has been a concern for condors.
By 2020, the total California condor population had grown to over 500, with more than half of those birds living in the wild after being released from the breeding program.
Condors mainly feed on carrion.

explanation: The population growth plus the fact that released captive-bred birds now live in the wild directly ties the recovery outcome to the breeding program.

stem:
In a research report, Dr. Oyelaran-Oyeyinka claims that using subtitles in the same language as a TV show's audio improves young children's reading fluency. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Many children's programs can be shown with optional same-language subtitles — for example, English audio with English captions. Dr. Oyelaran-Oyeyinka studies how media exposure affects early literacy. She argues that subtitles turn ordinary screen time into incidental reading practice.

choices.text: Some children's shows are produced in multiple languages.
Children who watched 30 minutes per day of subtitled programs for six months scored 18% higher on fluency tests than a matched group who watched the same shows without subtitles.
Captions are used by many adults for convenience.
Television is a popular form of entertainment in many households.

explanation: A controlled fluency-test comparison between subtitled and unsubtitled viewing directly measures the reading improvement the claim names.

stem:
A city planner argues that turning on-street parking spots into small parklets with seating increases the number of people who linger on a block. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Some urban blocks have replaced a few car-parking spaces with wooden platforms that hold benches, planters, and small tables. Supporters call these installations parklets and say they transform how people use the street. Skeptics say they mostly benefit nearby cafes.

choices.text: Parklets are usually built from wood or recycled materials.
Some parklets include planters full of flowers.
Pedestrian counts on blocks that added parklets showed the average number of people sitting or lingering rose from 6 to 34 during afternoon observation periods, while unchanged blocks stayed flat.
Parking spots in cities are sometimes hard to find.

explanation: The observation-period count directly measures lingering behavior on parklet blocks versus control blocks, which is precisely the outcome the claim predicts.

stem:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support the claim that adding a written peer-review step to middle school essay assignments leads to stronger final drafts?

Passage: In some classrooms, students submit a first draft, trade essays with a partner, and provide written feedback before revising. Other classrooms skip peer review entirely and go straight from first draft to teacher grading. A researcher wants to know whether the peer step actually improves the final essays.

choices.text: Many students say peer review feels awkward at first.

In a study comparing the two approaches, classrooms that included peer review produced final drafts that scored, on average, 14 points higher on a 100-point rubric than classrooms without peer review.
Peer review is also used in scientific publishing.
Essays in middle school typically run three to five paragraphs.

explanation: A direct rubric-score comparison between peer-review and non-peer-review classrooms measures the exact 'stronger final drafts' outcome the claim asserts.

stem:
A public-health writer claims that neighborhood walking groups help reduce blood pressure in adults over 50. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

Passage: Some community centers have organized walking groups that meet three times a week for an hour. The groups are free and are designed for older adults who may not otherwise exercise. The writer wants to know whether they produce measurable health changes.

choices.text: Walking is generally considered low-impact exercise.
Community centers often host a variety of activities.
Walking shoes are sold at many stores.

Participants over 50 who joined neighborhood walking groups showed an average decrease of 8 mmHg in systolic blood pressure after six months, while a comparison group of non-walkers showed no significant change.

explanation: The finding measures actual blood pressure change in walkers versus non-walkers, which is the exact outcome and population the claim names.

stem:
In an ecology paper, Dr. Aguilar argues: "The reintroduction of beavers to Rowan Creek is responsible for the recovery of the creek's native trout population, rather than the downstream pollution controls enacted the same year." Which finding, if true, would most directly support Dr. Aguilar's specific claim?

Passage: In 2012, conservationists released beavers into the headwaters of Rowan Creek, where their dams created deep, cool pools. That same year, new regulations cut industrial runoff entering the lower creek. Native trout populations, which had declined for decades, began to rebound. Scientists disagree about which change deserves the credit.

choices.text: Trout counts in the upper, beaver-dammed section of Rowan Creek rose by 240% between 2012 and 2020, while trout counts in the lower, pollution-controlled section without beaver dams rose by only 11%.
Beavers are known as 'ecosystem engineers' because they modify habitats.
Trout prefer cooler water temperatures.
Industrial runoff can carry heavy metals into waterways.

explanation: By isolating the upper section where beavers work from the lower section where only pollution controls apply, the finding ties the recovery specifically to beavers rather than to runoff rules — exactly the distinction Aguilar is drawing.

stem:
A historian claims: "The rise in literacy rates in 18th-century Scotland was driven primarily by the expansion of parish schools, not by the growth of private tutoring among wealthy families." Which finding, if true, would most directly support this specific claim?

Passage: In the 1700s, Scottish literacy rates climbed faster than in many neighboring regions. Two institutions grew during this period: a network of parish schools that taught reading to rural children for low fees, and private tutoring arrangements used by wealthier households. Historians have disagreed about which mattered more.

choices.text: Many Scottish families valued education as a path to social mobility.
Private tutoring was often conducted in the tutor's own home.

Parish records show that the sharpest literacy gains occurred in rural areas where parish schools opened, while literacy among the wealthy — who already used private tutors — changed little during the same period.
Scotland has a long tradition of producing respected writers and philosophers.

explanation: The finding compares gains in the parish-school population with the already-literate tutoring population, isolating parish schools as the driver — which is exactly the competing-causes distinction in the claim.

stem:
A researcher writes: "Tactile board games improve spatial-reasoning skills in elementary students more than equally engaging digital games that use the same rules." Which finding, if true, would most directly support this specific claim?

Passage: In many classrooms, students play games to build math and spatial skills. Some games use physical boards and wooden pieces; others use tablet versions with identical rules. A recent study tried to separate the effect of the physical materials from the game content itself.

choices.text: Students generally report enjoying both physical and digital versions of classroom games.
Digital games can automatically keep track of scores.

After six weeks, students randomly assigned to the tactile version of a spatial game scored 19% higher on a standardized spatial-reasoning test than students who played the identical digital version, even though both groups played for the same number of minutes and enjoyed their version equally.
Wooden game pieces are often made from sustainable materials.

explanation: The finding controls for time, rules, and engagement, leaving the physical-versus-digital format as the only meaningful difference — which is exactly what the claim isolates.

stem:
A labor economist claims: "The 2014 four-day workweek pilot at Bridgeton Manufacturing raised worker productivity not because employees simply rested more, but because the compressed schedule pushed managers to eliminate low-value meetings." Which finding, if true, would most directly support this specific claim?

Passage: In 2014, Bridgeton Manufacturing cut its workweek from five days to four without cutting pay. Total weekly output rose by 12% afterward. Analysts have offered several explanations, ranging from better rest to structural changes in how work was organized.

choices.text: Internal logs at Bridgeton showed that, after the switch, the number of scheduled meetings per employee dropped by 41%, and the output gains were concentrated in teams that had previously spent the most time in meetings.
Workers at Bridgeton reported feeling less tired after the switch.
Four-day weeks have been tried at other companies around the world.
Most workers at the company were paid hourly.

explanation: The finding ties the productivity gains specifically to meeting cuts — and does so most strongly for the teams that had the biggest meeting loads — which matches the meetings-based mechanism in the claim rather than a general rest effect.
